request.question: Who wrote the play "...but the clouds..." starring Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup?

title: Leo the Last
Make Mine Mink
Charlie Bubbles
Rockaby
Rough for Radio II
... but the clouds ...
Hotel Paradiso (film)
Footfalls
Ghost Trio (play)
Ronald Pickup
sentences: Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.
Make Mine Mink is a 1960 British comedy farce film directed by Robert Asher and featuring Terry-Thomas, Athene Seyler, Hattie Jacques and Billie Whitelaw.
The screenplay concerns a group of eccentric misfits who go on a spree, stealing mink coats for charity in a Robin Hood-style gang.
It was based on the play "Breath of Spring" by Peter Coke, and its sequels.
Seyer and Elspeth Duxbury reprised their stage roles from the London production of "Breath of Spring".
Charlie Bubbles is a 1967 British comedy-drama film starring Billie Whitelaw and Albert Finney, and also featuring a young Liza Minnelli.
It was listed to compete at the 1968 Cannes Film Festival, but the festival was cancelled due to the events of May 1968 in France.
Rockaby is a short one-woman play by Samuel Beckett.

It was written in English in 1980, at the request of Daniel Labeille, who produced it on behalf of "Programs in the Arts", State University of New York, for a festival and symposium in commemoration of Beckett's 75th birthday.
The play premiered on April 8, 1981 at the State University of New York at Buffalo, starring Billie Whitelaw and directed by Alan Schneider.
A documentary film, "Rockaby", by D. A. Pennebaker and Chris Hegedus records the rehearsal process and the first performance.
This production went on to be performed at the Annex at La MaMa Experimental Theatre Club, and, in December 1982, at the Cottesloe, Royal National Theatre, London.
Rough for Radio II is a radio play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in French in 1961 as Pochade radiophonique and published in "Minuit" 16, November 1975.
Beckett translated the work into English shortly before its broadcast on BBC Radio 3 on 13 April 1976.
Martin Esslin directed Harold Pinter (Animator), Billie Whitelaw (Stenographer) and Patrick Magee (Fox).
The English-language version was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Grove, 1976, Faber, 1977) as Radio II.
... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.
Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.
Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.
It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.
It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.
An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.
Hotel Paradiso is a Metrocolor 1966 film released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer in Panavision.
It was directed by Peter Glenville and based on the play "L'Hôtel du libre échange" by Maurice Desvallières and Georges Feydeau.
The film allowed Alec Guinness to reprise the role he had played in the London West End theatre production of Hotel Paradiso, which opened at the Winter Garden Theatre, Drury Lane, London.
on 2 May 1956.
Guinness played alongside Martita Hunt (Angelique), Irene Worth (Marcelle), Frank Pettingell (Cot), Kenneth Williams (Maxime) and Billie Whitelaw (Victoire).
Douglas Byng also reprised his part from the stage play.
Footfalls is a play by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in English, between 2 March and December 1975 and was first performed at the Royal Court Theatre as part of the Samuel Beckett Festival, on May 20, 1976 directed by Beckett himself.
Billie Whitelaw, for whom the piece had been written, played May whilst Rose Hill voiced the mother.
Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.
It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".
Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.
The play’s original title was to be Tryst.
"On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.
On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the play "...but the clouds..." starring Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup?
hop1_docs: ... but the clouds ... | ... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett.  Beckett wrote it between October–November 1976 “to replace a film of "Play" which the BBC had sent [him] for approval (and which he had rejected)” due to “the poor quality of the film”.  Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.  It was first broadcast on 17 April 1977 as part of a programme of three Beckett plays entitled ‘Shades’ on BBC2.  It was first published in "Ends and Odds" (Faber) 1977.  An early title for the piece was Poetry only love.

Ghost Trio (play) | Ghost Trio is a television play, written in English by Samuel Beckett.  It was written in 1975, taped in October 1976 and the first broadcast was on BBC2 on 17 April 1977 as part of the "Lively Arts" programme Beckett himself entitled "Shades".  Donald McWhinnie directed (supervised by Beckett) with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.  The play’s original title was to be Tryst.  "On Beckett’s notebook, the word was crossed out vigorously and the new title "Ghost Trio" written next to it.  On the title page of the BBC script the same handwritten title change can be found, indicating that it must have been corrected at the very last minute."
Leo the Last | Leo the Last is a 1970 British drama film directed by John Boorman, based on the play "The Prince" by George Tabori, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Billie Whitelaw.

Private Schulz | Private Schulz is a BBC television comedy drama serial set mostly in Germany, during and immediately after World War II.  It stars Michael Elphick in the title role and Ian Richardson playing various parts.  Other notable actors included Tony Caunter, Billie Whitelaw, Billy Murray and Mark Wingett.
Ronald Pickup | Ronald Alfred Pickup (born 7 June 1940) is an English actor who has been active in television and film since 1964.

Shadey | Shadey is a 1985 British comedy film directed by Philip Saville and starring Antony Sher, Billie Whitelaw and Patrick Macnee.  The screenplay concerns a man with clairvoyant qualities who is recruited by British intelligence for a secret mission.  The film grossed over $65,817.
Tangiers (1982 film) | Tangier is a 1982 American-Moroccan thriller film directed by Michael E. Briant and starring Ronny Cox, Billie Whitelaw and Glynis Barber.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "... but the clouds ..." — passage [1] states "... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett." (quote: "is a television play by Samuel Beckett." ) (passage [1]).
- Cast/director for that production — passage [1] states "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup." (quote: "Donald McWhinnie directed Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup.") (passage [1]).
- A second play with same cast — passage [2] shows "Ghost Trio is a television play... written in English by Samuel Beckett" and "Donald McWhinnie directed ... with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw." (quotes: "Ghost Trio is a television play" ; "directed ... with Ronald Pickup and Billie Whitelaw.") (passage [2]).

2) Ambiguities:
- Two candidate play pages appear in the passages with the same director and actors: "... but the clouds ..." (passage [1]) and "Ghost Trio" (passage [2]). Do not assume which is intended based only on shared cast/director.
- The question's title matches the exact token in passage [1], but the presence of Ghost Trio with identical cast could cause confusion in retrieval.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as given in the passages): "... but the clouds ..." (the television play).
- Precise missing fact needed to answer the question: the playwright credit for "... but the clouds ..." (i.e., who wrote the play). Passage [1] already contains a direct claim that the playwright is Samuel Beckett; this should be confirmed on the play page and by an authoritative source.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
1. Open the "... but the clouds ..." page (check lede/infobox and "Publication" or "Broadcast" sections) — search keywords: "... but the clouds ... Samuel Beckett lede/infobox".
2. Open "Ghost Trio (play)" page (lede and cast/director sections) to confirm it is a distinct play that also starred Billie Whitelaw and Ronald Pickup and to avoid conflation.
3. Open "Samuel Beckett" page (Works/Selected plays or bibliography) and the "Ends and Odds (Faber) 1977" publication entry to confirm authorship/publication details.
4. If needed, open the BBC "Shades" / broadcast entry (17 April 1977) to confirm which play(s) were broadcast and credited.

Final note: I have not given the final answer here; confirm the cited pages/sections above to finish the chain and produce the authoritative answer.
response.hop2_query: but the clouds television play playwright
hop2_docs: Cloud 9 (play) | Cloud 9 is a two-act play written by British playwright Caryl Churchill, workshopped with the Joint Stock Theatre Company in late 1978 and premiered at Dartington College of Arts, Devon, on 14 February 1979.

The Clouds | The Clouds (Ancient Greek: Νεφέλαι "Nephelai") is a Greek comedy play written by the celebrated playwright Aristophanes.  A lampooning of intellectual fashions in classical Athens, it was originally produced at the City Dionysia in 423BC and was not as well received as the author had hoped, coming last of the three plays competing at the festival that year.  It was revised between 420 and 417BC and was thereafter circulated in manuscript form.

Cordax | The cordax (Ancient Greek: Κόρδαξ ), was a provocative, licentious, and often obscene mask dance of ancient Greek comedy.  In his play "The Clouds," Aristophanes complains that other playwrights of his time try to hide the feebleness of their plays by bringing an old woman onto the stage to dance the cordax.  He notes with pride that his patrons will not find such gimmicks in his plays.

Charles Busch | Charles Louis Busch (born August 23, 1954) is an American actor, screenwriter, playwright and female impersonator, known for his appearances on stage in his own camp style plays and in film and television.  He wrote and starred in his early plays Off-off-Broadway beginning in 1978, generally in drag roles, and also acted in the works of other playwrights.  He also wrote for television and began to act in films and on television in the late 1990s.  His best known play is "The Tale of the Allergist's Wife" (2000), which was a success on Broadway.

Black Girl (play) | Black Girl is a play by American playwright J. E. Franklin.  It was first produced on public television in 1969, followed by an off-Broadway production in 1971.  It was later adapted by the playwright as a feature film that was released the following year.

Cecil Taylor (playwright) | Cecil Philip Taylor (1929 – 1981), usually credited as C.P. Taylor, was a Scottish playwright.  He wrote almost 80 plays during his 16 years as a professional playwright, including several for radio and television.  He also made a number of documentary programmes for the BBC.  His plays tended to draw on his Jewish background and his Socialist viewpoint, and to be written in dialect.

Pearson Playwrights' Scheme | Pearson Playwrights' Scheme (formerly Thames Television Theatre Writers Scheme) is a British organisation established in 1973 to support theatre writing.  It runs the Pearson Award for Best New Play.
response.summary_2: "... but the clouds ... is a television play by Samuel Beckett." The provided passages do not mention that play or Samuel Beckett; they discuss other works such as Cloud 9 — Caryl Churchill wrote that play — and The Clouds — Aristophanes wrote that play.
